content:
The idea of moving opposite the gradient to minimize a function dates to **Cauchy** (1847), who described **steepest descent** for solving systems of equations—take steps along the negative gradient of a quadratic error.

In the early 20th century, **Hadamard** and others refined gradient methods for analysis and computation. The modern **gradient descent** framing for smooth optimization matured mid-century alongside numerical linear algebra and convex analysis (**Dantzig**, **Kuhn**, **Tucker**).

**Rosenblatt**'s perceptron (1957) and later **Widrow–Hoff** delta rule used gradient-like updates for learning. **Rumelhart**, **Hinton** and **Williams** (1986) popularized **backpropagation**, making ∇L practical for layered networks and reviving interest in gradient-based training.

As datasets grew, **stochastic approximation** ideas from **Robbins and Monro** (1951) motivated **stochastic gradient descent**—same update rule, noisy gradient estimates—while batch GD remained the clean theoretical baseline.

Today batch GD is rarely used alone at LLM scale, but θ ← θ − η∇L(θ) is still the atomic step inside every optimizer and the reference against which SGD, momentum and adaptive methods are understood.

timeline.label: Steepest descent (Cauchy, 1847)
timeline.description: Iterative minimization by stepping along the negative gradient of a differentiable objective.

timeline.label: Stochastic approximation (Robbins–Monro, 1951)
timeline.description: Noisy gradient estimates enable large-scale iterative updates—precursor to SGD.

timeline.label: Backpropagation (1986)
timeline.description: Efficient ∇L computation for neural networks makes gradient descent practical for deep models.

timeline.label: Modern deep learning scale
timeline.description: Mini-batch SGD and adaptive optimizers dominate Gen AI training; batch GD remains the definitional update rule.
